data.title: Bunge Global: Awaiting Q3 Earnings As The Shares Move Lower
data.description:
I wrote about Bunge Global SA (NYSE:BG) on Seeking Alpha in mid-May 2024 when the shares were at the $104.83 level. I highlighted the $80 to $120 range in BG shares, concluding:

I am a scale-down buyer of Bunge Global SA during periods of price weakness, leaving room to add on further declines. BG is not only a leading agricultural producer and processor, but also a potential takeover or merger candidate that pays an attractive dividend that reduces risk while waiting for capital appreciation.

On October 22, 2024, BG shares had moved 14.8% lower to the $89.33 level. While a scale-down approach has yielded losses, BG shares could be near a bottom if the ten-year range holds. The company will report earnings at the end of this month.

BG shares decline since the July 2024 high

BG shares have made lower highs and lower lows since the July 26, $114.92 peak.

The six-month chart highlights the decline in BG shares over the past months, with the stock near the bottom end of the decade-long trading range.

Factor and Dividend Grades have mostly deteriorated

BG’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades moved mostly lower since mid-May 2024.

While BG’s valuation grade remains at an A since the mid-May 2024 article, growth fell from a D to a failing grade, profitability moved from B+ to B, and momentum dropped from a C+ to a D grade. The Revisions grade experienced a slight increase from a C+ to a B-.

Dividend grades fared slightly better, as the current $2.72 per share payout translates to a 3.04% yield.

Dividend safety fell from A- to B+, while dividend growth edged lower from B to B-. Consistency was consistent with a B rating, while the yield grade moved higher from a B+ to an A-.

The price action in the shares and consistency of the dividend caused the changes in Seeking Alpha’s factor and dividend grades.

Grain and oilseed prices have moved lower - Ethanol prices have declined

BG processes grains and oilseeds into food and fuel, making the company’s profits sensitive to the underlying commodity prices. Since May 16:

  • Nearby CBOT soybean futures prices declined 18.6% from $12.19 to $9.9175 per bushel.
  • Nearby CBOT corn futures prices declined 8.86% from $4.5700 to $4.1650 per bushel.
  • Nearby CBOT wheat futures prices declined 12.76% from $6.6025 to $5.7600 per bushel.
  • Nearby Chicago ethanol swap prices fell 13.00% from $1.8050 to $1.5700 per gallon wholesale.

While the leading grain and biofuel prices declined, sugar and soybean crush spreads moved higher since mid-May. Nearby ICE sugar futures rose 16.4% from 18.67 cents on May 16 to 21.73 cents per pound on October 22. The December soybean crush spread moved 73.4% higher from $1.0825 on May 16 to $1.8775 on October 22. While the crush and sugar have increased, grain, oilseed, and ethanol prices have declined, weighing on BG earnings margins.

BG earnings at the end of October

BG’s Q2 earnings were below the market’s consensus estimates:

With EPS and revenues below forecasted levels, the shares experienced pressure. BG will report Q3 earnings next week, with the current estimates above Q2 levels and revenues slightly lower:

The chart shows that the market expects higher EPS, but revenues are slightly lower than the Q2 $13.24 billion level at $13.20 billion.

The trend since 2020 remains bullish

As BG awaits Q3 earnings, the short-term trend remains bearish, with the stock moving towards the $80 per share support level. However, the longer-term chart has remained bullish since the 2020 low.

The five-year chart highlights the bullish price action that took BG shares 342.8% higher from the $29.00 March 2020 pandemic-inspired low to the $128.40 April 2022 high. CBOT wheat prices reached a record high in 2022, with corn and soybean futures rallying within pennies of their all-time peaks. Since then, the grain and oilseed futures markets have plunged, weighing on BG and other agricultural processors’ earnings.

Given BG’s significant exposure to Brazil, the price action in the U.S. dollar versus the Brazilian real exchange rate (USD:BRL) has also weighed on the shares.

The ten-year chart of the Brazilian real versus the U.S. dollar exchange rate shows the Brazilian currency is trading near the lows, weighing on earnings with Brazilian exposure.

As BG shares approach the $80 support level, I continue to favor a scale-down buying approach as the stock heads for the bottom end of its trading range. A positive earnings surprise on October 30 could ignite an overdue rally in BG, while further disappointment would likely cause the shares to challenge the $80 technical support. With BG near the bottom end of its range, I believe the company that processes the agricultural products that feed and increasingly power the world will hold the $80 level. Aside from earnings, the future path of least resistance of BG shares could depend on agricultural commodity prices and the Brazilian real’s strength or weakness against the U.S. dollar. BG is a proxy for agricultural commodities and Brazilian real exposure.

data.title: Northwest Bancshares: Over 6% Yield, But Downgrading To Hold Following Recent Rally
data.description:
Earnings of Northwest Bancshares, Inc. (NASDAQ:NWBI) will likely rebound after the second quarter's security restructuring. Loan growth will likely also return to last year's level after a disappointing quarter, leading to earnings growth in upcoming years. Overall, I'm expecting earnings to dip by 32% to $0.72 per share in 2024 before growing by 31% in 2025 to $0.94 per share. Compared to my last report on the company, I've reduced the earnings estimate for this year because of disappointing results for the second quarter of this year. The December 2025 target price is close to the current market price. Further, Northwest is offering an attractive dividend yield of 6.1%. Based on the total expected return, I'm downgrading Northwest Bancshares to a hold rating.

Restructuring to Lift, While Rate Cuts to Pressure the Margin

The net interest margin grew by 10 basis points during the second quarter of 2024 after four consecutive quarters of decline. The margin improvement was due to two factors: securities restructuring and deceleration of deposit migration.

During the second quarter, Northwest Bancshares sold off a large chunk of its securities portfolio at a loss of $39.4 million (pre-tax), which resulted in proceeds of $276 million, as mentioned in the earnings presentation. These outgoing securities had an average book yield of 1.79%. The management successfully reinvested most of the proceeds during the second quarter at a much higher yield of 6.0%. Although the restructuring was mostly completed in the second quarter, there was still some benefit left for the third quarter. The full-quarter impact of the restructuring will be seen in the third quarter.

The second factor for the margin's turnaround was the deposit migration slowdown. Previously, deposit migration had been a major reason for the margin's decline. A positive development during the second quarter was that the deposit mix deterioration slowed down. Management mentioned in the earnings presentation that the pace of volumes into higher-cost CDs and high-yield savings products began to slow, as shown in the table below.

I'm expecting deposit migration to become negligible soon because interest rate cuts will diminish the incentive of moving funds around and chasing yields. Therefore, the deposit migration factor will no longer affect the margin in the foreseeable future.

An important factor affecting the margin in the next year and a half is the interest rate down cycle. I'm expecting the Fed to cut the Fed funds rate by a further 25 basis points in the fourth quarter of 2024 and 75-100 basis points in 2025. This means that I'm expecting a total rate cut of 150-175 basis points from the third quarter of 2024 until the last quarter of 2025. The results of management's rate-sensitivity simulation model given in the 10-Q filing show that a 200-basis point rate cut could reduce the net interest income by 4.4% over twelve months.

Considering the recent restructuring and the ongoing rate down cycle, I'm expecting the net interest margin to have grown by three basis points in the third quarter of 2024. From the fourth quarter of 2024 until the end of 2025, I'm expecting the margin to dip by a single basis point each quarter.

Loan Growth to Improve as Payoffs Normalize

Northwest Bancshares' loan portfolio declined by 1.4% during the second quarter of the year, which was much worse than my expectations given in my last report on the company. Management mentioned on the conference call that a higher-than-expected level of runoffs in the portfolio was responsible for the decline. Management also mentioned that it was expecting runoffs to be less impactful going forward. Further, management expects loan growth to improve in the second half of this year as it mentioned on the conference call that it anticipates low-single-digit loan growth.

I, too, am optimistic about a recovery because the operating environment continues to be healthy. Northwest Bancshares' operations are concentrated in Pennsylvania. The company also has operations in Ohio, Indiana, and New York. As can be seen below, Pennsylvania's unemployment rate is currently doing better than not only the national average but also the state's own past.

Overall, I'm estimating the loan portfolio to have grown by 1.1% in the third quarter of 2024. Further, I'm expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 1.1% every quarter through the end of 2025. Compared to my last report on the company, I have reduced my loan balance estimate for 2024 because of the second quarter's disappointing performance. I haven't changed my growth estimate for 2025.

Moreover, I'm expecting deposits to grow in line with loans. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Financial Position FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net Loans 8,751 10,446 9,914 10,802 11,290 11,468 11,981
Growth of Net Loans 9.4% 19.4% (5.1)% 9.0% 4.5% 1.6% 4.5%
Other Earning Assets 838 1,578 2,317 2,099 1,858 1,859 1,935
Deposits 8,592 11,599 12,301 11,465 11,980 12,355 12,907
Borrowings and Sub-Debt 368 457 392 924 643 496 516
Common equity 1,353 1,539 1,584 1,491 1,551 1,563 1,580
Book Value Per Share ($) 12.8 12.8 12.5 11.8 12.2 12.3 12.4
Tangible BVPS ($) 9.3 9.5 9.4 8.7 9.2 9.3 9.4

Source: SEC Filings, Analyst's Estimates.

(In USD million unless otherwise specified).

Earnings to Rebound

Northwest Bancshares's earnings tanked in the second quarter due to large losses on the sale of securities. I'm expecting earnings to have rebounded in the third quarter to near the first quarter's level as the security restructuring is over. While subdued loan growth will drive earnings, slight margin pressure will likely restrict earnings growth from the fourth quarter of 2024 until the end of 2025.

Additionally, I'm making the following assumptions.

  1. I'm expecting the provision expense for expected loan losses to return to the 2023 level after remaining subdued in the first half of 2024. This is because I'm expecting loan growth to also return to the 2023 level. Further, I'm expecting the economy to remain stable.
  2. Excluding the losses on the sale of securities, I'm expecting non-interest income to remain rangebound. As mentioned above, I'm not expecting the company to realize any more losses through sales of securities.
  3. I'm expecting the efficiency ratio to return to the 1Q level after it was elevated in the second quarter due to the security restructuring.

Overall, I'm expecting the company to report earnings of $0.72 per share for 2024, down 32%, and $0.94 per share for 2025, up 31%. In my last report on the company, which was issued in July, I forecast earnings of $0.92 per share for 2024 and $0.94 per share for 2025. I've reduced my estimate for this year because the security restructuring took me by surprise. Also, I hadn't expected the loan portfolio to decline in size during the second quarter. I haven't changed my estimate for 2025 much as my outlook remains more or less the same.

Northwest Bancshares is scheduled to announce its third-quarter results on October 29, 2024. For the reasons mentioned above, I'm estimating the company to report earnings of $0.22 per share for the quarter.

The following table shows my income statement estimates.

Income Statement FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net interest income 360 392 391 421 436 425 441
Provision for loan losses 23 84 (12) 28 23 15 24
Non-interest income 99 132 143 111 114 75 114
Non-interest expense 296 347 345 330 352 369 378
Net income - Common Sh. 110 75 153 134 135 91 119
EPS - Diluted ($) 1.04 0.62 1.21 1.05 1.06 0.72 0.94

Source: SEC Filings, Earnings Releases, Analyst's Estimates.

(In USD million unless otherwise specified).

Vehicle Loans are the Main Source of Risk

The biggest source of risk for Northwest Bancshares is its exposure to vehicle loans, which make up around 17% of total loans. Vehicle loans are by nature quite risky because the collateral (car, motorbike, etc.) can get damaged, and the amount may not be entirely recoverable from insurance in all cases.

Following the securities restructuring, Northwest Bancshares has reduced the riskiness associated with the securities portfolio. Unrealized losses on the Available-for-Sale securities portfolio were down to $173.6 million at the end of June from $196.9 million at the end of December 2023. To put the latest amount in perspective, $173.6 million is 11% of the equity book value. I'm expecting these remaining unrealized losses to reverse as interest rates decline. I'm not expecting the company to sell any more securities, thereby turning unrealized losses into realized losses.

Dividend Yield of 6.1%

Northwest Bancshares is offering an attractive dividend yield of 6.1%, assuming the company maintains its quarterly dividend at the current level of $0.20 per share. The earnings and dividend estimates suggest a payout ratio of 85% for 2025, which is quite high compared to other banks. Nevertheless, such a high payout ratio is not unusual for Northwest as it has averaged a payout ratio of 82% over the last five years. Therefore, I think the chances of a dividend cut are negligible.

Furthermore, Northwest Bancshares has excess capital, so it does not need to cut back on dividends to build up capital. The company reported a total capital-to-risk-weighted assets ratio of 16.674% for the end of June, which was much higher than the regulatory requirement of 10.50%.

Downgrading to Hold

When I last wrote a report on Northwest Bancshares in July, the last closing price was $11.51. I determined a December 2024 target price of $12.20 in that report. As my target price and dividend yield suggested a good expected return, I adopted a buy rating. Since then, the stock price has rallied by an impressive 11%, to reach a level above my previous target price.

As we're now close to the end of this year, I've decided to roll over my target price to the end of next year. I'm using the historical price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") and price-to-earnings ("P/E") multiples to value the company. Northwest Bancshares has traded at an average P/TB ratio of 1.43x and an average P/E ratio of around 13.71x in the past, as shown below.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $9.40 gives a target price of $13.40 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 1.8% upside from the October 21 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/TB ratio.

P/TB Multiple 1.23x 1.33x 1.43x 1.53x 1.63x
TBVPS - Dec 2025 ($) 9.4 9.4 9.4 9.4 9.4
Target Price ($) 11.6 12.5 13.4 14.4 15.3
Market Price ($) 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2
Upside/(Downside) (12.4)% (5.3)% 1.8% 8.9% 16.0%

Source: Analyst's Estimates.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $0.94 gives a target price of $12.80 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 2.8% downside from the October 21 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/E ratio.

P/E Multiple 11.7x 12.7x 13.7x 14.7x 15.7x
EPS 2025 ($) 0.94 0.94 0.94 0.94 0.94
Target Price ($) 11.0 11.9 12.8 13.8 14.7
Market Price ($) 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2 13.2
Upside/(Downside) (17.0)% (9.9)% (2.8)% 4.3% 11.4%

Source: Analyst's Estimates.

Equally weighting the target prices from the two valuation methods gives a combined target price of $13.10, which implies a 0.5% downside from the current market price. Adding the forward dividend yield gives a total expected return of 5.6%.

Based on the updated total expected return, I think it's time to downgrade Northwest Bancshares to a hold rating. The earnings outlook is good, but it has already gotten priced in since the issuance of my last report.

data.title: Viatris Ahead Of Q3 Earnings: No Longer A 'Screaming' Bargain, But Still A Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview: Viatris' Price Volatility 2020 — Present Day Explained

I last covered Viatris Inc. (NASDAQ:VTRS) back in June, assigning the company's stock a “Hold” rating. As someone who bought a few shares shortly after Viatris — formed via a merger of Pfizer's (PFE) “Established Brands” division with the generics drugmaker Mylan — began trading in November 2020, I have closely followed the company's progress. I have shared multiple notes with Seeking Alpha readers, calling the company a “screaming buy” back in 2020.

There has been plenty of share price volatility during Viatris' short life as a listed company. Initial enthusiasm drove the share price >$22 per share, but the company's early performance and outlook disappointed the market. By April 2021, the stock had fallen in value to ~$13 per share.

The announcement in February 2022 that Viatris would sell its biosimilar assets to Indian Pharma Biocon in a $3.4bn led to another market sell-off, shares sinking to a value of ~$10. The biosimilars division was viewed as one of Viatris' best shots at revitalizing a business that earned the majority of its revenues selling patent expired drugs like Viagra, the statin Lipitor, and depression med Lyrica, hence the disappointment.

Analysts were quick to downgrade expectations and ratings, and by September 2022 shares had sunk in value to ~$8.5. The M&A deal for eye disease specialist Oyster Pharma, and guidance for full-year revenues in 2022 of $16.2 — $16.7bn created a brief bull market for Viatris stock, but despite a Q2 2023 earnings beat, and guidance for 2023 revenues of $15.5B — $16.0B; adjusted EBITDA of $5.0B — $5.4B, and free cash flow of $2.3B — $2.7B, a downgrade from S&P global ratings caused shares to slip once again, falling to <$9 per share in October 2023.

That date marked the nadir for Viatris — so far at least. In February this year, Viatris stock reached highs of >$13.5 per share, as various hedge funds disclosed holdings, perhaps attracted by a generous dividend, paying $0.12 per share, which rose >6% as shares traded lower.

2023 earnings, reported at the end of February, dropped the share price yet again. Viatris reported:

Total Revenues of $15.4 Billion, U.S. GAAP Net Earnings of $54.7 Million, Adjusted EBITDA of $5.1 Billion, U.S. GAAP Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities of $2.8 Billion and Free Cash Flow of $2.4 Billion

For 2024, the company guided for revenues of $15.2bn — $15.75bn of revenues, and $2.70–$2.85 in adjusted earnings per share (“EPS”), which disappointed Wall Street, despite management declaring a $1bn addition to its share repurchase fund.

Viatris stock nearly fell <$10 in June, but has mounted a slight recovery since. Q2 earnings, reported in August, showed total revenues for the half year of $7.44bn, down 2% year-on-year, adjusted gross profit of $4.4bn, compared to $4.6bn in the prior year period, and a net loss of $(213m), compared to a net profit of $489m in 1H23. Free cash flow fell 36% year-on-year, to $885m.

For 2024, guidance was adjusted to $14.98bn — $15.48bn of revenues, adjusted EBITDA of $4.7bn — $5.01bn, free cash flow to $2.26bn — $2.66bn, and adjusted EPS of $2.66 — $2.81 - a forward price to earnings (“PE”) ratio of ~4.3x, and forward price to sales (“P/S”) ratio of ~1.1x.

Analysis — Viatris Valuation Conundrum

As I have discussed in several notes before today, the investment opportunity in relation to Viatris looks extremely attractive at first glance. It has a P/S ratio of 1.1x and P/E ratio of ~4.3x, low enough to suggest Viatris stock is a “screaming buy.” For example, among the top 250 or so of the highest valuation stocks listed on the S&P 500 (SP500), only the banks JPMorgan (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Goldman Sachs (GS) have a lower P/E.

The problem is that, as we can see below, Viatris' revenues are falling, not rising, and so is its operating income:

As we can see above, in December 2021, Viatris earned $4.3bn in revenues, and $620m in operating income, and $4.2bn, and $863m respectively in the following quarter.

In Q1 2024, revenues were $3.65bn, and operating income $441m, and in Q2, $3.8bn and $361m respectively. In essence, then, Viatris' P/E and P/S ratios are low because the market expects to revenues and income to fall, not rise.

This is mainly because sales of Viagra, Lyrica, Lipitor, and Pfizer's other Established Brands are only going to fall now that the products have no patent protection. They have generally been supplanted by newer or cheaper products in their commercial markets.

This helps to explain, for example, the market's disappointment at the Biocon deal — considered a key long-term driver of new revenues, its removal from the business lessened the prospect of the company reversing the trend of falling revenues.

It should also be noted that Viatris began life as a company saddled with considerable debt. At the end of 2020, long-term debt was reported as $22.43bn. Falling revenues surely undermine the company's ability to pay off debt, but with so much debt to repay, there is little funding available for R&D, or bolt-on M&A deals that could raise revenues.

A Long-Term Project — Is Phase 1 Complete — Can Phase 2 Growth Succeed

The current CEO Scott Smith was appointed in 2023, replacing Michael Goettler, and Smith has been charged with leading “Phase 2” of Viatris' business transformation. Here is how Smith described Viatris' progress and future at the beginning of the company's Q2 earnings call with analysts:

Among the many accomplishments we have, integrated two global companies, simplified and streamlined the business by completing our divestitures, returned the base business to growth, strengthened our balance sheet through significant debt paydown, returned capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases and importantly, we've already started to build our portfolio of innovative assets.

As we move forward in the second half of the year and beyond, we want to accelerate growth and shareholder return by building on the strength of our base business with an expanding portfolio of innovative, best-in-class patent-protected assets that have the potential for meaningful revenue growth and patient impact.

It is certainly true that Viatris has paid off a substantial portion of its long-term debt — which stood at $14.73bn as of the end of Q2 — and achieved only its second quarterly uplift in revenues in Q2. New product revenues in Q2 were $210m, management reported.

Nevertheless, that is less than 6% of total revenues, and in Q2, both generics revenues of $1.42bn and Established Brands revenues of $2.63bn were down 3% year-on-year. It seems clear that Viatris needs to add more product revenues, and at a faster rate than it is currently doing, if Phase 2 of the company's life is to be a success.

Encouragingly, new product revenues are expected by management to be $500 — $600m in FY24 — the advantage Viatris has over other companies is its established global network of manufacturing, distribution, and marketing infrastructure, inherited from Pfizer / Mylan. The company earned ~$1.3bn in Europe in Q2, ~$1bn in the US, ~$580m in emerging markets, $350m in Japan, Australia, and New Zealand (“JANZ”), and $539m in China.

Viatris is bullish on its new products' ability to offset falling revenues in generics. Established brands — selatogrel and cenerimod, indicated for acute myocardial infarction (“AMI”) and systemic lupus erythematosus respectively, and a result of Viatris collaboration with Swiss drug developer Idorsia — are both considered to have “blockbuster” (>$1bn revenues per annum) potential by management.

Within complex injectables, Wixela and Breyna — both indicated for asthma — are new product launches, while Chief R&D officer Philippe Martin advised on the Q2 earnings call:

Yes, regarding the complex generic, I think, we -- as you can see on this presentation that we've provided, we have over 250 products in the pipeline that are either under development or under regulatory review. So we think we'll be -- we know we'll have a steady flow of complex generic coming in every year, this year, and 2025 and so on. So we feel confident about our complex generic pipeline.

The list of targets for complex injectables even includes Ozempic and Wegovy, the weight loss / diabetes drugs developed by Novo Nordisk (NVO) and would likely command billions in revenues if Viatris can find its way through the Big Pharma's patent thickets.

MS therapy Glatiramer, opioid sparing treatment meloxicam, and a botox generic are exciting “novel and complex” generics in development, and recently launched Tyrvaya in eye disease could command peak revenues ~$400m, analysts have speculated. Across its entire eye care portfolio, Viatris expects to find another >$1bn in long-term annual revenues.

My Conclusion: No Longer Reliant On Viagra For Growth!

Please excuse the pun, but the point I am inelegantly making is that Viatris looks likely to come out on the right side of the growth conundrum, in my opinion. The company seems likely to be able to offset falling sales of Established Brands and older generics with new product launches. Some of these offer long-term patent protection, while others are generic versions of drugs driving double-digit billion revenues today, i.e., Wegovy, Ozempic.

As mentioned, the global infrastructure, while expensive to maintain, hands Viatris a competitive advantage so long as its pipeline does deliver. There are multiple signs that it is indeed delivering, and will continue — the recent R&D day presentation highlights multiple “blockbuster” opportunities. Besides, Viatris has spent years trimming its unnecessary infrastructure “fat.” producing a slicker, more cost-effective business framework for the future.

Debt is being paid off while the dividend remains generous, and buybacks are being completed. Viatris is highly likely to remain profitable for the foreseeable future, thanks to the ballast that Established Brands bring — management is promising at least $2.3bn of cash flow per annum for the foreseeable future.

If I had to issue a caveat around the investment case, I would say there is not a lot of detailed guidance for 2025 or longer-term — no pledges around revenue growth, or margins, apart from the promise of the $2.3bn cash flow. That is, however, understandable is some ways, as Viatris is a company in transition, operating globally, selling hundreds of products and working on hundreds more.

Viatris also says in its Q2 earnings press release that:

In July, the Company brought to substantial completion all divestitures with the closing of its Over-the-Counter (OTC) business to Cooper Consumer Health, a leading European over-the-counter drug manufacturer and distributor.

Bearing all of these things in mind, my conclusion is that, while it has been a slow and sometimes dispiriting journey since 2020, Viatris has essentially done what it set out to do. That is to ease the debt burden, inspire growth, and keep shareholders happy with dividends and buybacks. However, the risk remains that the company continues to shrink, and therefore becomes less valuable, but this risk is becoming more and more substantially diminished, based on my research.

The next target I hope to see the business achieve is a $20bn market cap valuation (current valuation is ~$14bn), which I believe may be achievable by 2026 / 2027 — amid encouraging contributions from new products, and revenues >$15bn per annum (revenues may still shrink YOY over the coming years, but the key factor is the performance of newer products, and profitability).

I am therefore looking forward to Viatris' Q3 earnings due 7th November, and more so to the longer-term performance of the company and the new product launches to come.

Meanwhile, Pfizer can't seem to stand up for falling down, despite its massive COVID-19 vaccine revenues windfall in 2021 and 2022. The company might even regret discarding its established Brands. Despite realizing a tax benefit and unloading lots of debt on Viatris, these products have held up remarkably well in their markets. This should allow the spinout co. to develop into a business that is beginning to look in better shape than its parent.

data.title: Aker Carbon Capture: Upside Potential But No Catalysts
data.description:
Aker Carbon Capture ASA (OTCPK:AKCCF)(OSE ISIN:NO0010890304 which is the liquid issue) has changed considerably since we last covered it. As a review, it is one of the holdings of one of Norway's main blue-chips, Aker ASA (OTCPK:AKAAF), and is domestically well known in its own right. It still trades with a lot of liquidity despite the large and static Aker ASA shareholding, with a million or so average dollar-equivalent daily liquidity. It has now sold part of its business to Schlumberger (SLB), retaining a small stake and continuing its existence as a company with that now minority interest in the carbon capture business that it previously controlled. The value of that deal, and outstanding performance that may or may not be paid, as well as a put option to sell the remaining stake in the carbon capture business it just sold, appears to be greater than the current company's valuation. We think that there is still some gas left in this issue just on the basis of the NAV discount, but the market may be pricing in considerable time until major catalysts start kicking in, around 2025-2027, or maybe sooner this year once possible new investment plans are announced for the money they've been cashed out with, but it's unclear what the board will decide on. Based on the discount to cash per share of around 20% there is clearly an undervaluation, but catalysts are the issue here and bring down an expected annualized return to less exciting levels.

Info on the SLB Deal

The Q1 was the last quarter with any extensive disclosure. Q2 elaborated the situation as of the finalization of the deal, but lacked detailed disclosure, including an earnings call, until the board releases a strategy update on how to continue reporting and how to continue the Aker Carbon Capture ASA going concern, including possibly investing proceeds and taking some new stakes.

As said, Aker Carbon Capture is now just a vehicle for shares amounting to a 20% share in Aker Carbon Capture Holdings (ACCH is how it's being abbreviated). SLB established its position in ACCH paying Aker Carbon Capture ASA for its operations to be put into ACCH in the amount of 4.12 billion NOK, holding also onto 0.4 billion NOK in cash from the operation. There will also be 1.36 billion NOK more in performance payouts if certain milestones are hit in terms of orderbook and margin performance. This will depend on the modular solutions coming online at Brevik and fully at Twence, the Just Catch solution. Without the performance-based payment the ACC ASA net cash value per share appears to be at 7.45 NOK ($0.68) per share. This undervaluation in a 20% market to cash value discount only grows when considering the value of the remaining 20% ownership that Aker Carbon Capture ASA has in ACCH (reinforced somewhat by a put option), as well as with the possibility of the performance-based payouts.

Their formerly core operating business is now technically a discontinued operation and contained elsewhere within the J.V vehicle. Revenues are up more or less as much as they were up in Q1 in the Q2, but the recurring business coming from their carbon capture as a service model is still not coming in, which means the better industrial economics are only now dawning which is good as the business was running out of cash before SLB swooped in. The last phase as of the Q1, which to be clear was the last time the company disclosed detailed operational data, was underway at Twence before the sellable C02 starts getting produced. For Brevik it would be in late 2024. Outside of these two, other major projects are still a ways away from completion and are only earning some low margin integration and design revenues. Nonetheless, the scaling of revenues reflects the success in terms of building backlog. As of the Q1, the last time we got the data, backlog was up 10% YoY and was as high as 2.3 billion NOK, which is around a year of annualized run-rate revenues. They had been doing a lot of testing activity and pre-FEED work in "several" industrial locations as of the Q1.

Risks

The risks appear to be pretty limited for ACC, the stock in question, because it's basically just a cash box with a non-controlling stake in the ACCH J.V controlled by SLB. Of course, they have an interest in that business working out, but they've already been cashed out and the value of the ACC business as a cash box is greater than current market value, and we understand that their liability should be limited with respect to the ACCH which is a separate entity. They've also said that there is "no tax expense related to the disposed operations or the sale of the 80% shareholding" in the H1 report, which is good news. But without an announcement related to further payment considerations or what is going to be done with the current cash in the box, which is under de facto control of Aker ASA due to their north of 40% ownership in ACC, return periods could be protracted since markets probably want something to happen to the value of their "associate interest" in ACCH before rerating. Net cash is around 4.5 billion NOK as mentioned, and no major liabilities are present.

We don't have a lot of disclosure about what is happening in the J.V anymore, and presumably it doesn't matter too much, but it was burning around 210 million NOK per quarter. Cash was around 902 million NOK that quarter, so it was ticking down fast. 400 million NOK was also retained by ACC since then when it spun it off. It will definitely need new cash, and probably needs it right now with 400 million NOK removed. How it will be financed now with SLB controlling it is not clear, but "SLB will fund the business plan and working capital fluctuations" (from the Q1 presentation) for the next three years (the lock-up period) so it's not likely just ACC's problem, and we understand there most likely won't be dilution. The real concern is around what management of ACC will do with the cash, considering corporate governance concerns with Aker ASA owning 42%, and the risk of the money being dead for too long and the return period too protracted.

Conclusions

The upside is around 20% to cash ($0.68). 52% (PT of $0.82 for the US listing) when considering the value of the remaining 20% ownership in ACCH based on SLB's consideration for the 80% of the business. PTs get higher with performance-based compensation assessed towards 2027. Also, there's another important detail:

After a lock-up period of three years, Aker Carbon Capture will be entitled to sell its stake in ACCH to SLB. The put option price will be based on the fair market value of the combined business with a floor of NOK 1 billion for the retained 20% stake and a ceiling at two times this price.

Egil Fagerland, CEO of Aker Carbon Capture ASA

The lock-up period ends in around 2027, which would be when focus on the valuation of its ACCH holding possibly picks up for the benefit of closing the NAV discount of ACC. 1 billion NOK is what SLB valued the operation at when they engineered this J.V. deal to make ACCH.

The issue is that with possible catalysts only coming in 3 years from now, annualized returns from this level won't be smooth, and only really amount to around 15% per year into 2027 when considering the better case scenario of the current SLB valuation of the J.V. persisting (the put option provides some amount of hope of that, but it is conditional). The upside gets lower on just a cash basis, assuming that the ACCH stake has no value.

The only other catalyst hope is that the board decides to do something with the newfound cash that would excite the markets, but it's unclear what they will do, although we will find out when they release a strategy update.

Aker Carbon Capture comes with risks. Its holding represents an interest in a business that is still unprofitable operationally and dependent on subsidies. While there might be some alpha here, it's not as much as it might seem when you start thinking about the higher risk profile against the 7% equity return long-term benchmark due to the risk of a protracted return period. It doesn't help that Aker ASA has considerable control over ACC as well, so the position vis-a-vis the rest of the shareholder is unclear.

data.title: FX And U.S. Election Cheat Sheet
data.description:
By Francesco Pesole, Frantisek Taborsky & Chris Turner

This article is part of our US Election coverage and focuses on near-term dynamics. We discuss election scenarios and medium-term currency market implications in detail in “US election guide for the FX market”.

The latest polls suggest the upcoming US election is a close call, but financial and betting markets have recently swung more in favour of a Donald Trump win. We will walk you through the numbers in key battleground states, discuss how we see market positioning in the two weeks leading to the election, and explore the potential initial reactions in FX.

The 2024 US swing states cheat sheet

Battleground numbers

The table above summarises what polls and betting markets are telling us about the upcoming Presidential election for seven battlegrounds (or “swing”) states and the national outlook. According to the latest poll aggregates, 226 electoral college votes are either solidly or leaning Democrat, while 219 are for the Republicans. The seven battleground states listed are closely contested, with leads within the statistical margin of error. To reach the 270 electoral college win threshold, Harris needs to secure 44 of the 73 available swing state votes; Trump needs 51 – assuming all lock/lean states don’t flip.

"Trump is marginally ahead in the swing states"

If the latest poll averages (third column) prove correct, Trump wins the election with Republican lock/lean votes (219) + Arizona (11) + Georgia (16) + North Carolina (16) + Pennsylvania (19) = 281 electoral college votes. Harris would need to win all states where she is already ahead in the polls (including Michigan, Nevada and Wisconsin) plus another 13 electoral college votes from the swing states where Trump is currently leading. That means securing Arizona (11) alone wouldn’t be enough, and Harris would need to win either Georgia (16), North Carolina (16) or Pennsylvania (19), with the latter widely seen as the state that can tip the balance.

Polls versus bets

While there is no simple market measure of the Harris/Trump implied probability, betting markets are often taken as a benchmark. In the table above, we see that the traditional bookmaker (Betfair) odds highly favour Trump. We also looked at the CFTC-regulated portal Kalshi, where it is possible to buy/sell the equivalent of binary options on either candidate.

Betting markets have given Trump a better chance than the polls

Kalshi’s election winner market only started in October, so the chart above uses data from PredictIt, an analogue election-betting website. Both Kalshi and PredictIt implied probability of a win has generally been leaning more in favour of Trump relative to what poll-based simulations were suggesting. Remember that these portals work similarly to stock markets, where the price is determined by buying and selling volume.

The rise in bets on Trump in such markets is probably a reflection of both polls and some hedging, considering a Trump win is seen as the more impactful event for markets. Incidentally, in both 2016 and 2020, the Republicans fared markedly better than polls and betting markets had anticipated, and that can also explain why betting markets have been favouring Trump this time.

FX implied volatility to rise into the vote

In the last two US presidential elections, the cost of FX hedging increased significantly in the two weeks leading up to the vote. One way to measure this is the ratio of one-month implied volatility to one-month historical volatility. A ratio above 1.0 suggests that markets anticipate larger spot movements in the upcoming month compared to the previous 30 days.

Rise in FX implied volatility may have only just started

As you can see above, the implied/historical volatility ratio increased markedly in the 14 days preceding the 2020 and 2016 election days for G10 dollar crosses. We expect a similar dynamic this time, especially considering the latest polls are narrowly favouring Trump, whose win can generate larger volatility across the currency market. Since that hedging demand should mostly be related to protection for a Trump-led dollar rally, we think the balance of risks remains skewed to a stronger USD into the vote.

"FX liquidity can dry up close to the vote"

In particular, we remain concerned that some de-risking in the FX market can lead to poorer liquidity conditions. The Norwegian krone is often a good indicator of such conditions, given it is the least liquid G10 currency. Despite good fundamentals, we suspect EUR/NOK can trade back above 12.0 before the US election.

FX market isn't fully pricing in Trump

Since mid-October, markets have gradually priced in more Trump risk, mainly through higher US rates, pressure on emerging market currencies and some USD strength. This means that a potential “relief” rally following a Harris win can now be larger and hit the dollar harder.

That said, the FX market is not fully pricing in a Trump victory. The dollar’s strength is still mostly a function of stronger US data, and EUR/USD (currently at 1.082) is trading less than 1% below its short-term fair value. An undervaluation of at least 2% (the 1.5 standard deviation) would be needed to conclude there is a Trump-related risk premium embedded in the pair.

"EUR/USD is not embedding a Trump risk premium"

Over the recent period, EM currencies have sold off on the strong dollar but have not – yet – substantially underperformed G10 currencies. This may change. Most vulnerable could be CEE currencies, which have double exposure through EUR crosses and large export openness, translating into sensitivity to potential changes in global trade in the case of a Trump victory.

At the moment, HUF seems the most exposed within the region, where the central bank does not have many options to defend the currency and the market has already outpriced any rate cuts over the last two weeks. On the other hand, the CZK and PLN seem more defensive and may also benefit from a possible relief in case of a Harris victory.

Elsewhere, things could have been worse for the Latin and Asian currencies were it not for recent Chinese stimulus measures. Yet both blocs still look vulnerable to further losses under a full Trump 2.0.

FX market reaction to Trump's 2016 win

Gauging the initial FX impact

The 2020 Presidential election was somewhat unique, and results were significantly delayed due to the very high number of mail-in ballots and the Republicans contesting the count in some states. In the table at the top of this article, we summarised the point at which the key swing states were called by the Associated Press in the past three elections. It’s worth noting that Biden’s win was not officially called until the Saturday after the vote (so four days after).

There is probably a greater risk of a delay in the count and official results for this election compared to any other election before 2020, and news agencies may well be more careful in calling a state or the Presidency than in previous instances. Most swing states are on the East Coast, where polls close between 7PM and 8PM ET, but the high volume of mail-in ballots can cause delays. The preliminary results in Pennsylvania can have one of the deepest market impacts as this is seen as a must-win state for Harris, but local regulation allows mail-in votes to be counted only on Election Day, which can lead to a lengthy count.

Anyway, there is a good possibility the FX market will “call” the winner already on the night between 5 November and 6 November. We expect the initial reaction to mostly entail protectionism-related trades. This means the wider swings can be seen in AUD and NZD in the G10 space; in EM, Asian currencies and MXN will be particularly sensitive.

Which currencies are most exposed to Trump tariffs?

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Blackstone: Time To Take Profits
data.description:
Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) had better-than-expected results for the third quarter last week as its profits grew YoY and its asset management business is firing on all cylinders.

Blackstone’s assets under management reached $1.1 trillion, and investors received the quarterly earnings release very well, sending the stock to new highs.

Though Blackstone’s 3Q24 earnings were great, and the asset manager is enjoying some decent momentum in terms of asset inflows, I don’t think the valuation makes much sense here with a distributable earnings multiple of 42x.

Consequently, I don’t see a particularly compelling risk/reward relationship and have decided to take profits.

My Rating History

My last stock classification on Blackstone was Hold. Growing assets under management and a potential investment opportunity in distressed real estate justified a hold classification at that particular moment in time, in my view, though I was already cautious at the time about the asset manager’s valuation. Blackstone’s third quarter earnings led to a new stock high on Friday, which is when I closed my position in the asset manager.

Growth In Assets Under Management And Distributable Earnings

Blackstone produced $1.4 billion in distributable profits in the third quarter, up 1% YoY. The asset manager had a particularly good quarter in terms of its management and advisory business, which produced 8% higher earnings compared to the year ago period. In total, this segment contributed $1.79 billion in earnings in the third quarter. Weaker performance was booked in the fee-related performance business, which is where profits fell 4% YoY.

Blackstone did particularly well in the third quarter in terms of reeling in new assets. Total assets under management went up 10% YoY to $1.11 trillion, while fee-earning assets under management climbed 12% YoY to $820.5 billion.

Inflows were strong, particularly in credit & insurance as well as in private equity, where the asset management company gathered $21.4 billion and $10.2 billion in assets, respectively. Strong asset collection is an indicator of platform strength and could point to growing profits for the asset manager in the future.

Taking into account that Blackstone’s valuation is already quite stretched based on distributable earnings, I think that this might actually be a good time to lock in profits for investors.

Dividend Pay-Out Metrics

Blackstone earned $1.01 per share in distributable earnings in the third quarter, up 7% YoY on growth in the asset management and advisory segment primarily.

The asset manager paid out $0.86 per share in dividends in the third quarter, which equates to an 85% dividend pay-out ratio. This is equal to the amount of distributable earnings the investment company paid out in the last twelve months as well.

Blackstone’s dividend here is very well-covered by distributable earnings and while I myself was invested in part because of the dividend, the dividend yield alone is not a sufficiently good reason to pay a sky-high premium for the asset management company.

With Blackstone’s stock price skyrocketing, the company’s dividend yield has compressed down to just 2%. The yield stood at 4.6% when I first presented Blackstone as an investment opportunity back in February 10, 2023.

Unsustainable Valuation

I have been cautious on Blackstone for valuation reasons before, but the present multiple likely implies negative return potential in the future, in my view. Based on run-rate 3Q24 distributable earnings, Blackstone is selling for a profit multiple of 43x, which seems excessively high.

I estimated in my earlier presentation about the asset manager that Blackstone could earn $4.10-4.15 per share this year in distributable earnings. Based on this range, the profit multiple slips to 42x, which is still excessively high.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR), for which I estimated full-year profit potential of $3.80-4.00 per share in 2024, is selling for $139.81 at the time of writing, which reflects back to us a 36x leading (2024) distributable earnings multiple.

Both asset managers are quite expensive at this point, which makes buying them at this point kind of uncompelling.

Since Blackstone is now also much more expensive than KKR, I have decided to sell my BX stock on Friday and lock in all my profits.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Turn Out In My Favor

Blackstone reported robust results for 3Q24 that showed a broad acceleration in the company’s asset management business. Institutions are still very comfortable putting money into Blackstone’s asset management segment, which could propel the stock price higher, so there is definitely the risk of missing out on more upside.

With that said, I do have problems with the valuation multiple here and asset management businesses also tend to be cyclical, meaning they perform well when the stock market is performing well also.

Taking into account the cyclical component inherent in Blackstone’s asset management business, I see even much less reason to own the stock moving forward.

On the flipside, in case the stock market reaches new highs and investors are comfortable putting money into Blackstone’s funds, then there is a possibility for Blackstone to report strong asset and earnings growth which may make the stock cheaper on a distributable profit basis.

My Conclusion

The third quarter was clearly a good one for Blackstone from a business perspective: Profits grew, the asset manager is witnessing asset inflows across its businesses and distributable earnings, the ultimate measure of success, were up as well.

Blackstone’s distribution earnings rose 7% per share YoY, and the company did well in terms of growing its assets under management.

Taking into account that Blackstone’s stock has performed very well and that it now sells for more than 42x estimated 2024 distributable earnings, I think the risk/reward relationship is not that favorable. The dividend yield also contracted to just 2%, down from 4.6% when I first recommended Blackstone.

data.title: Nordea: Profitability Slowly Normalizing, But Shares Cheap At 7.3x Earnings
data.description:
Shares of pan-Nordic banking giant Nordea (OTCQX:NRDBY)(OTCQX:NBNKF) have lagged wider European financials (EUFN) over the past year, albeit having still returned ~20% thanks to the boost from higher interest rates.

I first covered Nordea back in April, rating it a "Buy" as the bank's mid/high teens return on equity ("ROE") more than offset the stock's modest premium to book value. These shares have been a fairly disappointing performer since then, again lagging broader European financials by a double-digit margin.

Recent financial results indicate that Nordea is still over-earning relative to management's ROE target, but not by enough to leave the stock expensive when profitability normalizes. I remain bullish on the bank and keep my previous rating in place.

Q3 Overview

In the company's third quarter, Nordea reported net income of €1.27 billion, down ~6% year-on-year and ~3% quarter-on-quarter. EPS of €0.36 (~$0.39 per ADS) was slightly more resilient, falling ~5% year-on-year as buybacks provided a modest tailwind.

While net income is falling, this is coming off a high base, as the steep rise in interest rates significantly widened Nordea's net interest margin ("NIM") in 2022 and 2023. At the same time, credit quality has also remained very resilient, and this combination has basically led to ROE remaining near cycle-highs. Given that, some degree of earnings normalization is to be expected, so declining net income is not concerning as such.

Starting with interest income trends, Nordea's NIM was 177bps last quarter, flat year-on-year but down around 6bps sequentially as interest rate cuts weigh on deposit margins. Again, this decline basically represents a base effect more than anything else, and NIM remains ~60bps higher than it was before European policy rates started to rise in 2022:

At the same time, the current interest rate environment hasn't yet spurred a meaningful increase in demand for credit. Nordea's retail and business/corporate lending balances were basically static last quarter, although deposit balances did show better performance, rising by ~4%. With margins contracting slightly and volumes still subdued, net interest income ("NII") was also fairly weak, falling ~1% year-on-year to €1.88 billion.

Elsewhere, fee income was stronger, increasing ~4% year-on-year to €774 million. Nordea has a relatively significant asset management ("AM") business, and AM fees constitute its largest source of non-interest income (and ~15% of total income). Thanks to positive flows and strong markets, total AUM rose ~15% year-on-year to €412.4 billion last quarter, while AM fee income rose ~5% to €432 million. Business lines adjacent to its core banking business also reported growth, with payments and card income rising ~8% year-on-year to €150 million as customer activity picked up.

Costs were a bit of a mixed bag. At €1.33 billion, operating expenses were up around 11% year-on-year and 4% sequentially in Q3, outpacing both revenue and wider inflation. This drove a decline in pre-provision operating income, which fell 4% year-on-year to €1.69 billion. Operating expenses were somewhat muddied by certain one-offs (namely M&A integration expenses and a U.S. compliance fine), and "core" expense growth was better, increasing by ~1% year-on-year. Nordic banks in general are well-known for being efficient, and Nordea's cost/income ratio was still under 45% in Q3 even including these one-offs.

In contrast, loan loss charges continue to come in surprisingly light, padding earnings. Credit charges were just €51 million last quarter, or roughly 6bps of total loans, boosted by a modest release of management's macro overlays. Stage 3 loans (i.e. non-performing) did tick up 10bps sequentially to 105bps, and while this will be something to monitor going forward, the NPL rate remains at low levels relative to historical levels. Since credit charges remain below management's 10bps through-the-cycle target, this line will likely be a modest headwind to earnings/profitability in the future.

Looking at particular areas of investor concern, commercial real estate is not as significant at Nordea compared to other major Scandinavian banks, though at ~10% of total loans it is still meaningful. Average loan-to-value ratios are around 50% in all of Nordea's major markets, so the bank has a significant cushion even if property prices were to fall substantially.

Turning to the balance sheet, Nordea remains very well capitalized, with its CET1 ending last quarter at 15.8%. While down from over 17% at initial coverage, model changes were expected to drive an increase in risk-weighted assets, so this decline was anticipated. More importantly, the current CET1 remains around 80bps ahead of management's 15% goal, implying around €1.2 billion in surplus capital.

Shares Can Still Be Cheap

Looking ahead, management expects NII to trough next year or possibly in 2026, as deposit margins eventually stabilize and volume growth returns to modest positive territory.

The quicker we see the rate cuts, the sooner we'll see the trough. And that could be in '25, given the sort of spillover effect of rate cuts, may go over into 2026.

Ian Smith, Nordea Group Chief Financial Officer

As mentioned above, earnings normalization is not a problem as such. Nordea's ROE was still 16.8% last quarter and 17.5% year-to-date. With €8.63 in average year-to-date book value per share ("BVPS")(~$9.34 per ADS), the current €10.94 share price (~$11.85 per ADS) only puts Nordea on a roughly 7.3x multiple of YTD earnings (i.e. a ~1.27x BVPS multiple divided by a ~17.5% ROE). Said in plainer English, the market is already pricing in lower future earnings.

Given that, the key questions are, one, where Nordea's profitability will settle across the cycle, and two, how expensive the stock is relative to this. Management's longer-term ROE target is ~15%, which would be a decline compared to 2023 (~16.9%) and YTD 2024 (~17.5%), but materially above the ~10% ROE that the bank averaged before interest rates started rising in 2022. Given the forward curve still points to modestly positive Eurozone rates at the end of the easing cycle, a structural step-up in profitability does not strike me as an unreasonable assumption.

Assuming a ~15% ROE, Nordea stock would trade for ~8.1x mid-cycle earnings based on its Q3 BVPS multiple (i.e. 1.22x divided by 15%). Theoretically that means the shares would yield over 12% if management simply distributed 100% of net income to shareholders. That also means the stock could still be quite cheap even if earnings do fall modestly.

Nordea will probably retain some of its earnings to fund growth. The bank's operating footprint is quite evenly spread between the four major Nordic countries. Given its size and current market shares, long-term growth in line with nominal GDP is probably a fair assumption – and realistically that will be somewhere around 2% based on past figures for these countries.

Assuming a ~15% ROE, Nordea would only need to retain around 15% of its through-the-cycle earnings to fund this (a 15% ROE multiplied by 15% retained income implies roughly 2% growth), leaving the rest available for dividends and buybacks. Note that this isn't entirely theoretical - based on a ~15% ROE management does intend to pay out most of its net income to investors in 2024 and 2025:

That gives the shares a roughly ~12-13% long-term total returns potential even on lower mid-cycle earnings, with that consisting of ~10-11% from capital returns (~12.3% earnings yield multiplied by ~85%) plus approximately 2% from underlying growth.

Conclusion

Nordea's net income is falling, albeit this is normal as the macro environment normalizes from peak-cycle conditions. More importantly, this is more than reflected in the stock's current valuation, with Nordea trading for ~7.3x year-to-date 2024 earnings and ~8.1x potential mid-cycle earnings based on management's 15% ROE target. With even the latter figure implying attractive double-digit returns potential, Nordea shares have a reasonable margin of safety in the event that profitability undershoots this. I remain bullish and leave my initial "Buy" rating unchanged.
